# Handoff — experiment-infrastructure spec review

**Situation.** A draft spec for experimental-infrastructure (setup,
artifact, lifecycle conventions for running framework-touching trials)
has been written at `experiments/infrastructure/setup-and-artifacts.md`.
You are picking up to refine it with Sean.

**Next move.** Open the spec, read it end-to-end, and start the
refinement conversation. Ask Sean which sections he wants to scrutinize
first; don't preemptively rewrite or summarize.

## Anti-orientation

- **Do NOT** re-derive the design conversation that produced the spec.
  Substantive design decisions are captured in the spec itself.
- **Do NOT** open `.scratch/cost-optimization-landscape.md`,
  `.scratch/p3-handoff-rig1-t52.md`, or
  `experiments/X011-context-debt/artifacts/2026-04-30-implementation-tail-analysis.md`
  unless Sean references them. They're context, not load-bearing for
  the spec review.
- **Do NOT** scan the click tree at startup beyond the standard
  orientation. The relevant clicks are listed below.

## Background context (read only if Sean refers to it)

- **Driver:** P3 (engine-pipeline-decomposition; click `c-modxxtu6`,
  cost-optimization landscape idea #15) needs empirical confirmation
  for several sub-questions, starting with Q4 (orientation suppression).
  This infrastructure is the prerequisite for those empirical trials.
- **What's settled:** the three-repo separation (sanctum, nexus
  experiment branches, short-lived codex forks), one-guild-per-trial
  model, sanctum as canonical record, manifest-driven setup, file-per-
  table stacks dump format, per-commit codex history capture.
- **What's open:** the spec's "Open implementation questions" section
  lists 8 framework-mapping questions that need answering before the
  setup/archive/cleanup scripts can be built. None are blockers for the
  spec review itself.
- **What's NOT this:** building the actual scripts. That's a follow-on
  task once the spec is settled.

## Pointers

- **The spec:** `experiments/infrastructure/setup-and-artifacts.md`
- **Driving click:** `c-modxxtu6` (P3 / idea #15) — has a child click
  pointing at this spec as a prerequisite.
- **Earlier session:** transcript at session
  `8dd39ccd-28cc-45eb-8cdb-447d0169b353` (this draft was produced near
  the end of that session; refer if Sean wants context on a specific
  decision).

## What Sean said when handing off

> "I will review the spec and continue refining it with clean context."

So expect: Sean has read or is reading the spec, has questions and/or
disagreements with specific sections, and wants targeted refinement —
not a re-pitch of the whole design.

1	# Experimental infrastructure: setup, artifacts, lifecycle
2	
3	**Status:** draft (2026-04-30) — pending Sean's review.
4	
5	## Purpose
6	
7	This document specifies the infrastructure for running framework-side
8	experiments — trials that need a controlled codex, controlled framework
9	state, isolated session recording, and reproducible setup. The immediate
10	driver is P3 (engine-pipeline-decomposition; click `c-modxxtu6`), whose
11	sub-questions (orientation suppression, trigger heuristics, granularity,
12	cost confirmation) all require running real implementer sessions in
13	isolated conditions before the architecture can be designed empirically.
14	Subsequent experiments touching framework or commission-pipeline behavior
15	will reuse the same infrastructure.
16	
17	The infrastructure does **not** govern paper analyses (instruments run
18	against existing transcript archives) — those need no setup. It governs
19	trials that **execute new commissions** under controlled conditions.
20	
21	## Design principles
22	
23	1. **Three-repo separation.** The sanctum, the framework (nexus), and the
24	   codex live in three repos with distinct persistence rules. Each artifact
25	   has one canonical home.
26	2. **One guild per trial.** Every trial gets its own fresh guild and its
27	   own short-lived codex fork. This eliminates trial-isolation problems
28	   inside the guild's books — every row in the guild DB belongs to this
29	   trial by construction.
30	3. **Sanctum is the canonical record.** Methodology, briefs, prompts,
31	   captured execution data, and analysis live in the sanctum. The other
32	   two repos (nexus branches, codex forks) are reproducibility breadcrumbs.
33	4. **Reproducibility via SHA pinning.** Every framework dep, every codex
34	   base, every plugin version is pinned to a SHA in a per-trial manifest
35	   that lives in the sanctum.
36	5. **Disposable execution surfaces.** Codex forks and live guild dirs are
37	   short-lived. They're created at trial start, archived to sanctum at
38	   trial end, and deleted. Re-running an experiment doesn't depend on
39	   long-lived disposable state.
40	
41	## Architecture
42	
43	### The three repos
44	
45	| repo | role | persistence |
46	|---|---|---|
47	| **Sanctum** (`nexus-mk2`) | methodology, manifests, prompts, captured artifacts, analysis, findings | indefinite; canonical research record |
48	| **Nexus framework** | only the framework changes that produce experimental variance. One branch per experiment (not per trial) — a single experiment-wide framework state that all variants in that experiment share. | indefinite; archived branches |
49	| **Codex fork** | short-lived complete fork of the codex repo, one per trial. The fork's `main` branch is the trial's working ref; commits there are the rigs' actual edits. | short-lived; captured-then-deleted |
50	
51	The codex fork lives in **the same GitHub org as the upstream codex** to
52	avoid permission concerns. Forks are named
53	`experiment-<X-num>-<slug>-trial-<NNN>-<variant>` to keep them
54	traceable while alive.
55	
56	### Lifecycle
57	
58	```
59	[manifest.yaml in sanctum]
60	        │
61	        ▼
62	   experiment-init   → creates nexus branch (if needed), forks codex,
63	                        creates guild dir, generates guild.json +
64	                        package.json, snapshots setup to sanctum,
65	                        stubs trial README
66	        │
67	        ▼
68	   [run trial]       → post commissions; rigs execute; sessions record
69	        │
70	        ▼
71	   experiment-archive → dumps stacks DB to JSON-per-table, captures
72	                        codex fork commits as diffs, copies session
73	                        transcripts, writes archive-manifest
74	        │
75	        ▼
76	   experiment-cleanup → deletes codex fork (gh repo delete) and the
77	                        live guild dir; refuses to run if archive
78	                        wasn't completed
79	```
80	
81	The nexus experiment branch is **not** deleted at cleanup — it persists
82	in the nexus repo as a reproducibility breadcrumb.
83	
84	## Trial manifest format
85	
86	Each trial is parameterized by a YAML manifest stored in the sanctum
87	under the experiment dir. The manifest is the canonical specification
88	of the trial's experimental conditions.
89	
90	```yaml
91	# experiments/X<NNN>-<slug>/artifacts/trial-NNN-<variant>/manifest.yaml
92	
93	experiment: X<NNN>-<slug>      # experiment slug; matches the parent dir
94	trial: NNN                     # trial number, zero-padded (001, 002, ...)
95	variant: <variant-slug>        # what makes this trial different from others
96	
97	nexus:
98	  branch: experiment/X<NNN>-<slug>     # branch in /workspace/nexus
99	  base_sha: <40-char SHA>              # the SHA the branch was created from
100	                                       # (for new experiments) or expected to be
101	                                       # at (for additional trials in the same
102	                                       # experiment)
103	
104	codex:
105	  upstream_org: shardworks
106	  upstream_repo: nexus                 # or whatever the codex repo is
107	  base_sha: <40-char SHA>              # codex SHA the trial starts from
108	
109	plugins:                               # exact plugin pinning for the
110	                                       # experimental package.json
111	  - name: '@shardworks/clerk-apparatus'
112	    ref: <SHA>
113	  - name: '@shardworks/animator-apparatus'
114	    ref: <SHA>
115	  # ...
116	
117	guild:
118	  laboratory_sink: artifacts/trial-NNN-<variant>/laboratory/
119	                                       # sanctum-relative; resolved to
120	                                       # absolute path at init time
121	
122	prompts_dir: artifacts/trial-NNN-<variant>/prompts/
123	                                       # sanctum-relative; copied/symlinked
124	                                       # into the guild's role-instruction
125	                                       # location at init time
126	
127	archive_extras:                        # optional: tables to include beyond
128	                                       # the default stacks export
129	  - cdc_events
130	```
131	
132	### Schema notes
133	
134	- `experiment` and `variant` slugs use lowercase kebab-case, no whitespace.
135	- `trial` is zero-padded to three digits.
136	- All paths in the manifest are sanctum-relative (relative to the
137	  experiment dir). The init script resolves them to absolute paths.
138	- All SHAs are full 40-char (no abbreviated forms). The init script
139	  refuses ambiguous refs.
140	
141	## Setup script — `experiment-init.sh`
142	
143	```
144	bin/experiment-init.sh --manifest <path-to-trial-manifest.yaml>
145	```
146	
147	### Steps
148	
149	1. **Validate manifest.** Required fields present, SHAs are 40-char and
150	   resolve, paths exist, slug not already in use (no existing guild dir
151	   at the target location).
152	2. **Ensure nexus branch.** If `experiment/X<NNN>-<slug>` doesn't exist
153	   in `/workspace/nexus`, create it from `nexus.base_sha`. If it exists,
154	   verify it points at the manifest's SHA — refuse to proceed on drift.
155	   Push to origin if newly created.
156	3. **Fork the codex.** `gh repo fork <upstream_org>/<upstream_repo>
157	   --org <upstream_org> --fork-name experiment-<X-num>-<slug>-trial-<NNN>-<variant>`.
158	   The fork's `main` is the trial's working branch, started at
159	   `codex.base_sha`. (If the fork's `main` HEAD doesn't match `base_sha`
160	   — e.g., the upstream advanced — explicitly reset the fork's `main`
161	   to the manifest's SHA before proceeding.)
162	4. **Create guild dir.** `/workspace/experiments/<X-num>-<slug>-trial-<NNN>-<variant>/`
163	   with whatever subdirs the framework requires.
164	5. **Generate `guild.json`.** Codex URL points at the fork; codex SHA
165	   pinned per the manifest. Laboratory sink resolved to the absolute
166	   sanctum path.
167	6. **Generate `package.json`.** Plugins pinned exactly per the
168	   manifest's `plugins` list. (The exact form of pinning depends on
169	   the framework's package layout; the script consumes the list and
170	   writes the appropriate `dependencies` block.)
171	7. **Init the guild.** Run `nsg init` (or whatever the current command
172	   is) against the guild dir.
173	8. **Copy prompts** from the manifest's `prompts_dir` into the guild's
174	   role-instruction location. Symlink rather than copy if the framework
175	   supports it (so prompt edits in sanctum take effect without a
176	   re-init), but verify the framework actually re-reads on each session
177	   spawn before relying on this.
178	9. **Snapshot to sanctum.** Write `experiments/X<NNN>-<slug>/artifacts/trial-NNN-<variant>/setup-snapshot.yaml`
179	   capturing every resolved SHA, fork URL, paths, manifest copy, init
180	   timestamp, and the script invocation arguments. This is the
181	   methodology breadcrumb.
182	10. **Generate trial README stub** at `experiments/X<NNN>-<slug>/artifacts/trial-NNN-<variant>/README.md`
183	    with the **Setup** section auto-filled and the rest as headers
184	    waiting for the experiment author to fill in.
185	11. **Print next steps.** "Guild initialized at
186	    `/workspace/experiments/...`. Post commissions via
187	    `nsg commission-post --guild ...`. When the trial is done, run
188	    `bin/experiment-archive.sh <trial-dir>` to capture state, then
189	    `bin/experiment-cleanup.sh <trial-dir>` to remove disposable surfaces."
190	
191	### Idempotency
192	
193	Re-running init on an existing guild dir refuses to proceed (refuse with
194	a clear error, do not overwrite). Force-re-init is not supported — the
195	intended flow is `archive + cleanup + re-init`, never silent overwrite.
196	
197	## Archive script — `experiment-archive.sh`
198	
199	```
200	bin/experiment-archive.sh <trial-slug>
201	```
202	
203	Where `<trial-slug>` is the form `X<NNN>-<slug>-trial-<NNN>-<variant>`,
204	or a path to the live guild dir. The script resolves to the
205	corresponding sanctum artifact dir.
206	
207	### Steps
208	
209	1. **Read the trial's setup-snapshot** from sanctum to find the guild
210	   dir, fork URL, etc.
211	2. **Dump the stacks DB.** Walk every table in the guild's stacks DB
212	   and write each as JSON to `artifacts/trial-NNN-<variant>/stacks-export/<table>.json`.
213	   The dump format is defined below ("Stacks export format").
214	3. **Capture codex fork commits.** Walk the fork's `main` branch from
215	   `codex.base_sha` to HEAD, extract each commit's diff and metadata,
216	   write to `artifacts/trial-NNN-<variant>/codex-history/`. Format
217	   defined below ("Codex history format").
218	4. **Copy session transcripts.** Pull session jsonls from
219	   `~/.claude/projects/<draft-dir>/<sessionId>.jsonl` into
220	   `artifacts/trial-NNN-<variant>/transcripts/`. The session-to-rig
221	   mapping comes from the stacks dump (which is captured in step 2).
222	5. **Write archive manifest** at `artifacts/trial-NNN-<variant>/archive-manifest.yaml`
223	   summarizing what was captured: counts per table, count of commits,
224	   count of transcripts, archive timestamp, script invocation.
225	6. **Print next steps.** "Archive complete. Run
226	   `bin/experiment-cleanup.sh <trial-slug>` to delete disposable surfaces.
227	   Verify the archive contents at `<sanctum-path>` before running cleanup."
228	
229	The script is idempotent: re-running overwrites the prior dump (with a
230	warning). Cleanup refuses to run if archive-manifest is missing or stale
231	relative to the live state.
232	
233	## Cleanup script — `experiment-cleanup.sh`
234	
235	```
236	bin/experiment-cleanup.sh <trial-slug>
237	```
238	
239	### Steps
240	
241	1. **Verify archive completion.** Refuse to run if
242	   `artifacts/trial-NNN-<variant>/archive-manifest.yaml` doesn't exist
243	   or its timestamp is older than the guild dir's most recent
244	   modification (suggests work happened after archive — re-archive
245	   before cleanup).
246	2. **Delete the codex fork.** `gh repo delete <fork> --confirm`. Fail
247	   loud if the fork has unmerged content the script doesn't recognize
248	   from the archive (rare, but the safety check matters).
249	3. **Delete the guild dir.** `rm -rf /workspace/experiments/<trial-dir>`.
250	4. **Print confirmation.** "Trial <slug> cleanup complete. Sanctum
251	   artifacts preserved at `<path>`."
252	
253	The script is **not idempotent** — re-running on a cleaned trial errors
254	("nothing to clean up").
255	
256	## Artifact layout
257	
258	```
259	experiments/X<NNN>-<slug>/
260	├── spec.md                                # the experiment's research spec
261	└── artifacts/
262	    ├── trial-001-<variant-slug>/
263	    │   ├── manifest.yaml                  # the canonical trial config
264	    │   ├── setup-snapshot.yaml            # init-time snapshot
265	    │   ├── archive-manifest.yaml          # archive-time summary
266	    │   ├── README.md                      # trial-level prose doc
267	    │   ├── prompts/                       # variant-specific prompts
268	    │   │   └── ...
269	    │   ├── transcripts/                   # per-session jsonls
270	    │   │   └── <sessionId>.jsonl
271	    │   ├── stacks-export/                 # JSON dump per table
272	    │   │   ├── writs.json
273	    │   │   ├── rigs.json
274	    │   │   ├── sessions.json
275	    │   │   ├── clicks.json
276	    │   │   └── schema-version.txt
277	    │   ├── codex-history/                 # per-commit diffs + meta
278	    │   │   ├── commits-manifest.yaml
279	    │   │   └── 0001-<short-sha>-<slug>.diff
280	    │   ├── laboratory/                    # Laboratory's observation sink
281	    │   │   └── <whatever Laboratory writes>
282	    │   └── analysis/                      # trial-specific analysis (if any)
283	    │       └── <date>-<slug>.md
284	    │
285	    ├── trial-002-<variant-slug>/          # parallel structure per trial
286	    │   └── ...
287	    │
288	    └── analysis/                          # cross-trial analysis
289	        └── <date>-comparison.md
290	```
291	
292	### Notes
293	
294	- **Trial dir name is `trial-NNN-<variant-slug>`.** NNN is zero-padded;
295	  variant slug is lowercase kebab-case.
296	- **`trial-NNN-<variant>/analysis/`** is for analysis specific to one
297	  trial; **`artifacts/analysis/`** is for cross-trial comparison.
298	- **`trial-NNN-<variant>/laboratory/`** is where Laboratory writes its
299	  observations during the trial. Path is captured in the manifest's
300	  `guild.laboratory_sink`.
301	- **Findings go in `experiments/X<NNN>-<slug>/findings.md`** at the
302	  experiment root, not in any trial dir. The findings doc consolidates
303	  across trials and answers the experiment's research question.
304	
305	## Stacks export format
306	
307	One JSON file per table, each containing an array of row objects.
308	
309	```json
310	[
311	  {
312	    "id": "w-...",
313	    "type": "mandate",
314	    "title": "...",
315	    "body": "...",
316	    "status": "open",
317	    "createdAt": "2026-...",
318	    "...": "..."
319	  },
320	  { "id": "w-...", "...": "..." }
321	]
322	```
323	
324	### File-per-table choice
325	
326	Per-table beats per-row for our use case:
327	
328	- Typical row sizes are 1-10KB; per-row overhead (filesystem blocks,
329	  git blob entries) eats meaningful space at this scale.
330	- Row counts per trial are tens to hundreds — table files stay
331	  manageable.
332	- We don't need row-level git history (dumps are snapshots, not
333	  append-only).
334	- Analysis instruments load the table once and iterate.
335	
336	### Carve-out for large content
337	
338	If any single row carries content >50KB (e.g., a writ body that's a
339	50KB brief), the dump can extract it to `stacks-export/blobs/<sha>.<ext>`
340	and replace the inline content with a reference field
341	(`body_ref: "blobs/<sha>.md"`). Only do this when actually needed; the
342	default is inline.
343	
344	### Schema versioning
345	
346	`stacks-export/schema-version.txt` records the framework SHA the dump
347	was taken against. Future readers use this to interpret the row schema.
348	
349	### Default scope
350	
351	A fresh guild contains only the trial's data, so the default dump is
352	**every table, every row**. No scope filtering needed. The manifest's
353	optional `archive_extras` field lets a trial opt into additional tables
354	beyond the framework defaults (e.g., raw CDC event log) if the
355	experiment specifically needs them.
356	
357	## Codex history format
358	
359	```
360	codex-history/
361	├── commits-manifest.yaml
362	├── 0001-<short-sha>-<slug>.diff
363	├── 0002-<short-sha>-<slug>.diff
364	└── ...
365	```
366	
367	### `commits-manifest.yaml`
368	
369	```yaml
370	base_sha: <40-char>            # the trial's codex base
371	head_sha: <40-char>            # the fork's main HEAD at archive time
372	commits:
373	  - seq: 0001
374	    sha: <40-char>
375	    parent: <40-char>
376	    short: <8-char>
377	    subject: 'reckoner: sweep vision-keeper references'
378	    author: 'implementer'
379	    timestamp: '2026-...'
380	    rig_id: 'r-...'              # cross-ref into rigs.json
381	    session_id: '<sessionId>'    # cross-ref into sessions.json + transcripts
382	    writ_id: 'w-...'             # cross-ref into writs.json
383	    diff_file: '0001-<short>-<slug>.diff'
384	  - seq: 0002
385	    ...
386	```
387	
388	### Diff files
389	
390	Standard git format-patch / git format style; one file per commit. The
391	filename's seq prefix preserves order; the short SHA + slug aids
392	human navigation.
393	
394	## Trial README format
395	
396	Each trial dir has a `README.md` capturing the trial's narrative.
397	
398	```markdown
399	# Trial <NNN> — <variant slug>
400	
401	## What this trial tests
402	<one paragraph: which hypothesis or variant config; what we expect>
403	
404	## Setup
405	- Nexus branch: <branch> @ <SHA>
406	- Codex fork: <URL> @ <SHA>
407	- Plugin deps: see `setup-snapshot.yaml`
408	- Variant config: <pointer to prompts/ or framework changes>
409	
410	## Methodology
411	<what commissions were posted, in what order, what was measured>
412	
413	## Results
414	<metrics, comparisons, surprises; reference analysis/ files>
415	
416	## Verdict
417	<confirmed / refuted / inconclusive, with one-paragraph rationale>
418	```
419	
420	The init script auto-fills **Setup** with resolved SHAs and paths. The
421	experiment author fills the rest.
422	
423	## Trial discipline
424	
425	- **One X### = one experiment with one research question.** Use the
426	  X### namespace freely. Don't overload one X### with multiple
427	  experiments.
428	- **Within one experiment, partition by trial.** Variants of the same
429	  hypothesis live as separate trial dirs.
430	- **Heuristic for trial vs. experiment:** same hypothesis → trial; new
431	  hypothesis → new experiment.
432	- **Old X### items with multiple experiments** stay as historical
433	  exceptions. Don't rationalize. Apply the discipline going forward.
434	
435	## Naming conventions
436	
437	| thing | format | example |
438	|---|---|---|
439	| Experiment slug | `X<NNN>-<lowercase-kebab>` | `X016-p3-orientation-suppression` |
440	| Nexus experiment branch | `experiment/<experiment-slug>` | `experiment/X016-p3-orientation-suppression` |
441	| Codex fork repo | `experiment-<experiment-slug>-trial-<NNN>-<variant>` | `experiment-X016-p3-orientation-suppression-trial-001-strong-prompt` |
442	| Trial dir slug | `<experiment-slug>-trial-<NNN>-<variant>` | (used in `/workspace/experiments/` and as the cleanup arg) |
443	| Trial artifact dir | `trial-<NNN>-<variant>/` | `trial-001-strong-prompt/` |
444	
445	Variant slugs use lowercase kebab-case, descriptive but short.
446	
447	## Worked example: X016 P3 orientation suppression
448	
449	(Hypothetical — illustrates how the pieces fit together.)
450	
451	### Experiment
452	
453	- **Number:** X016
454	- **Slug:** `X016-p3-orientation-suppression`
455	- **Research question:** Can a fresh implementer session, given a
456	  hand-crafted 30K-token handoff, produce productive work in <5 turns?
457	- **Spec location:** `experiments/X016-p3-orientation-suppression/spec.md`
458	
459	### Trials
460	
461	Three prompt variants tested against the same brief (rig 1's docs sweep,
462	handoff at T52):
463	
464	| trial | variant | what differs |
465	|---|---|---|
466	| 001 | `strong-prompt` | imperative anti-orientation directives ("Do NOT re-read X"), evidence anchors |
467	| 002 | `mild-prompt` | permissive framing ("X is known-good") |
468	| 003 | `monolithic-baseline` | no handoff; runs the brief from scratch as control |
469	
470	### Per-trial flow
471	
472	1. Author writes `manifest.yaml` for trial-001-strong-prompt.
473	2. `bin/experiment-init.sh --manifest .../trial-001-strong-prompt/manifest.yaml`
474	   creates the nexus branch (if not already), forks the codex, generates
475	   the guild, snapshots setup, stubs the README.
476	3. Post the brief commission via `nsg commission-post --guild
477	   /workspace/experiments/X016-p3-orientation-suppression-trial-001-strong-prompt
478	   --brief .../prompts/handoff-strong.md`.
479	4. Rig executes; sessions record; Laboratory writes observations to
480	   the trial's laboratory dir.
481	5. When the trial completes, `bin/experiment-archive.sh
482	   X016-p3-orientation-suppression-trial-001-strong-prompt` dumps stacks,
483	   captures codex commits, copies transcripts, writes archive manifest.
484	6. Author fills out the trial README with methodology + results +
485	   verdict.
486	7. `bin/experiment-cleanup.sh X016-p3-orientation-suppression-trial-001-strong-prompt`
487	   deletes the fork and guild dir.
488	8. Repeat for trials 002 and 003.
489	
490	### Cross-trial analysis
491	
492	`experiments/X016-p3-orientation-suppression/artifacts/analysis/2026-MM-DD-orientation-comparison.md`
493	runs an instrument that compares orientation-tax metrics across the
494	three trials' transcripts and stacks dumps; produces the answer to the
495	research question. Findings consolidate to
496	`experiments/X016-p3-orientation-suppression/findings.md`.
497	
498	## Open implementation questions
499	
500	These are unresolved in this draft and need addressing before the
501	infrastructure is built:
502	
503	1. **`nsg init` invocation.** What does the current command look like?
504	   Does it accept a path argument for the guild dir, or does it create
505	   a guild in the current dir? The setup script needs the right
506	   incantation.
507	2. **Plugin pinning mechanism.** How does the framework's `package.json`
508	   express plugin pinning today? By workspace path, by npm version, by
509	   git ref? The script needs to generate the right shape.
510	3. **Prompts copy vs. symlink.** Does the framework re-read role
511	   instructions on each session spawn (allowing symlink-and-edit), or
512	   only at guild init (requiring copy + re-init for changes)? Affects
513	   how prompts variation works during a trial.
514	4. **Stacks dump primitive.** Does the framework provide a clean dump
515	   command that produces JSON-per-table, or does the archive script
516	   need to query the DB directly? If the latter, format-stability
517	   becomes a concern across framework versions.
518	5. **Laboratory sink config.** How is the Laboratory's output
519	   destination configured today — guild.json field, env var, plugin
520	   prop? The init script needs to wire it deliberately.
521	6. **Session transcript location.** The transcripts live in
522	   `~/.claude/projects/<draft-dir>/<sessionId>.jsonl` for production
523	   today. For experimental guilds with non-default draft locations, do
524	   the transcripts still go there? Affects the archive script's
525	   transcript-copy step.
526	7. **`gh` org permissions.** Does the experimental setup user have
527	   permissions to fork into the upstream codex's org and to create/delete
528	   forks programmatically? If not, fork-creation needs human-in-loop.
529	8. **Convention for `archive_extras` table names.** Should the manifest
530	   reference table names directly, or is there a higher-level set of
531	   "raw event log," "queue snapshots," etc. that the script knows how
532	   to extract?
533	
534	These are pragmatic questions about how the framework's existing
535	primitives map to what the script needs to do. They're each
536	straightforward once we look at the framework code; surfacing them
537	now so the next pass can resolve them.
538	
539	## Future considerations
540	
541	- **Multi-fork experiments.** The current spec defaults to one fork per
542	  trial. Some experiments may need multiple parallel forks (e.g.,
543	  comparing prompt variants concurrently to save calendar time). Design
544	  is compatible with this — just init multiple manifests.
545	- **Long-lived experiments.** Some experiments may run trials over
546	  weeks (e.g., observing P3 behavior across many real commissions). The
547	  cleanup discipline needs adjusting for these — possibly an interim
548	  "snapshot" step that captures state without deleting the fork.
549	- **Cross-experiment comparison.** When two experiments answer related
550	  questions (e.g., X016 P3 orientation, X017 P3 trigger heuristics),
551	  cross-experiment analysis lives in `experiments/analysis/` (or wherever
552	  the sanctum convention places it). Out of scope for this spec.
553

# Experimental infrastructure: setup, artifacts, lifecycle

**Status:** draft (2026-04-30) — pending Sean's review.

## Purpose

This document specifies the infrastructure for running framework-side
experiments — trials that need a controlled codex, controlled framework
state, isolated session recording, and reproducible setup. The immediate
driver is P3 (engine-pipeline-decomposition; click `c-modxxtu6`), whose
sub-questions (orientation suppression, trigger heuristics, granularity,
cost confirmation) all require running real implementer sessions in
isolated conditions before the architecture can be designed empirically.
Subsequent experiments touching framework or commission-pipeline behavior
will reuse the same infrastructure.

The infrastructure does **not** govern paper analyses (instruments run
against existing transcript archives) — those need no setup. It governs
trials that **execute new commissions** under controlled conditions.

## Design principles

1. **Three-repo separation.** The sanctum, the framework (nexus), and the
   codex live in three repos with distinct persistence rules. Each artifact
   has one canonical home.
2. **One guild per trial.** Every trial gets its own fresh guild and its
   own short-lived codex fork. This eliminates trial-isolation problems
   inside the guild's books — every row in the guild DB belongs to this
   trial by construction.
3. **Sanctum is the canonical record.** Methodology, briefs, prompts,
   captured execution data, and analysis live in the sanctum. The other
   two repos (nexus branches, codex forks) are reproducibility breadcrumbs.
4. **Reproducibility via SHA pinning.** Every framework dep, every codex
   base, every plugin version is pinned to a SHA in a per-trial manifest
   that lives in the sanctum.
5. **Disposable execution surfaces.** Codex forks and live guild dirs are
   short-lived. They're created at trial start, archived to sanctum at
   trial end, and deleted. Re-running an experiment doesn't depend on
   long-lived disposable state.

## Architecture

### The three repos

| repo | role | persistence |
|---|---|---|
| **Sanctum** (`nexus-mk2`) | methodology, manifests, prompts, captured artifacts, analysis, findings | indefinite; canonical research record |
| **Nexus framework** | only the framework changes that produce experimental variance. One branch per experiment (not per trial) — a single experiment-wide framework state that all variants in that experiment share. | indefinite; archived branches |
| **Codex fork** | short-lived complete fork of the codex repo, one per trial. The fork's `main` branch is the trial's working ref; commits there are the rigs' actual edits. | short-lived; captured-then-deleted |

The codex fork lives in **the same GitHub org as the upstream codex** to
avoid permission concerns. Forks are named
`experiment-<X-num>-<slug>-trial-<NNN>-<variant>` to keep them
traceable while alive.

### Lifecycle

```
[manifest.yaml in sanctum]
        │
        ▼
   experiment-init   → creates nexus branch (if needed), forks codex,
                        creates guild dir, generates guild.json +
                        package.json, snapshots setup to sanctum,
                        stubs trial README
        │
        ▼
   [run trial]       → post commissions; rigs execute; sessions record
        │
        ▼
   experiment-archive → dumps stacks DB to JSON-per-table, captures
                        codex fork commits as diffs, copies session
                        transcripts, writes archive-manifest
        │
        ▼
   experiment-cleanup → deletes codex fork (gh repo delete) and the
                        live guild dir; refuses to run if archive
                        wasn't completed
```

The nexus experiment branch is **not** deleted at cleanup — it persists
in the nexus repo as a reproducibility breadcrumb.

## Trial manifest format

Each trial is parameterized by a YAML manifest stored in the sanctum
under the experiment dir. The manifest is the canonical specification
of the trial's experimental conditions.

```yaml
# experiments/X<NNN>-<slug>/artifacts/trial-NNN-<variant>/manifest.yaml

experiment: X<NNN>-<slug>      # experiment slug; matches the parent dir
trial: NNN                     # trial number, zero-padded (001, 002, ...)
variant: <variant-slug>        # what makes this trial different from others

nexus:
  branch: experiment/X<NNN>-<slug>     # branch in /workspace/nexus
  base_sha: <40-char SHA>              # the SHA the branch was created from
                                       # (for new experiments) or expected to be
                                       # at (for additional trials in the same
                                       # experiment)

codex:
  upstream_org: shardworks
  upstream_repo: nexus                 # or whatever the codex repo is
  base_sha: <40-char SHA>              # codex SHA the trial starts from

plugins:                               # exact plugin pinning for the
                                       # experimental package.json
  - name: '@shardworks/clerk-apparatus'
    ref: <SHA>
  - name: '@shardworks/animator-apparatus'
    ref: <SHA>
  # ...

guild:
  laboratory_sink: artifacts/trial-NNN-<variant>/laboratory/
                                       # sanctum-relative; resolved to
                                       # absolute path at init time

prompts_dir: artifacts/trial-NNN-<variant>/prompts/
                                       # sanctum-relative; copied/symlinked
                                       # into the guild's role-instruction
                                       # location at init time

archive_extras:                        # optional: tables to include beyond
                                       # the default stacks export
  - cdc_events
```

### Schema notes

- `experiment` and `variant` slugs use lowercase kebab-case, no whitespace.
- `trial` is zero-padded to three digits.
- All paths in the manifest are sanctum-relative (relative to the
  experiment dir). The init script resolves them to absolute paths.
- All SHAs are full 40-char (no abbreviated forms). The init script
  refuses ambiguous refs.

## Setup script — `experiment-init.sh`

```
bin/experiment-init.sh --manifest <path-to-trial-manifest.yaml>
```

### Steps

1. **Validate manifest.** Required fields present, SHAs are 40-char and
   resolve, paths exist, slug not already in use (no existing guild dir
   at the target location).
2. **Ensure nexus branch.** If `experiment/X<NNN>-<slug>` doesn't exist
   in `/workspace/nexus`, create it from `nexus.base_sha`. If it exists,
   verify it points at the manifest's SHA — refuse to proceed on drift.
   Push to origin if newly created.
3. **Fork the codex.** `gh repo fork <upstream_org>/<upstream_repo>
   --org <upstream_org> --fork-name experiment-<X-num>-<slug>-trial-<NNN>-<variant>`.
   The fork's `main` is the trial's working branch, started at
   `codex.base_sha`. (If the fork's `main` HEAD doesn't match `base_sha`
   — e.g., the upstream advanced — explicitly reset the fork's `main`
   to the manifest's SHA before proceeding.)
4. **Create guild dir.** `/workspace/experiments/<X-num>-<slug>-trial-<NNN>-<variant>/`
   with whatever subdirs the framework requires.
5. **Generate `guild.json`.** Codex URL points at the fork; codex SHA
   pinned per the manifest. Laboratory sink resolved to the absolute
   sanctum path.
6. **Generate `package.json`.** Plugins pinned exactly per the
   manifest's `plugins` list. (The exact form of pinning depends on
   the framework's package layout; the script consumes the list and
   writes the appropriate `dependencies` block.)
7. **Init the guild.** Run `nsg init` (or whatever the current command
   is) against the guild dir.
8. **Copy prompts** from the manifest's `prompts_dir` into the guild's
   role-instruction location. Symlink rather than copy if the framework
   supports it (so prompt edits in sanctum take effect without a
   re-init), but verify the framework actually re-reads on each session
   spawn before relying on this.
9. **Snapshot to sanctum.** Write `experiments/X<NNN>-<slug>/artifacts/trial-NNN-<variant>/setup-snapshot.yaml`
   capturing every resolved SHA, fork URL, paths, manifest copy, init
   timestamp, and the script invocation arguments. This is the
   methodology breadcrumb.
10. **Generate trial README stub** at `experiments/X<NNN>-<slug>/artifacts/trial-NNN-<variant>/README.md`
    with the **Setup** section auto-filled and the rest as headers
    waiting for the experiment author to fill in.
11. **Print next steps.** "Guild initialized at
    `/workspace/experiments/...`. Post commissions via
    `nsg commission-post --guild ...`. When the trial is done, run
    `bin/experiment-archive.sh <trial-dir>` to capture state, then
    `bin/experiment-cleanup.sh <trial-dir>` to remove disposable surfaces."

### Idempotency

Re-running init on an existing guild dir refuses to proceed (refuse with
a clear error, do not overwrite). Force-re-init is not supported — the
intended flow is `archive + cleanup + re-init`, never silent overwrite.

## Archive script — `experiment-archive.sh`

```
bin/experiment-archive.sh <trial-slug>
```

Where `<trial-slug>` is the form `X<NNN>-<slug>-trial-<NNN>-<variant>`,
or a path to the live guild dir. The script resolves to the
corresponding sanctum artifact dir.

### Steps

1. **Read the trial's setup-snapshot** from sanctum to find the guild
   dir, fork URL, etc.
2. **Dump the stacks DB.** Walk every table in the guild's stacks DB
   and write each as JSON to `artifacts/trial-NNN-<variant>/stacks-export/<table>.json`.
   The dump format is defined below ("Stacks export format").
3. **Capture codex fork commits.** Walk the fork's `main` branch from
   `codex.base_sha` to HEAD, extract each commit's diff and metadata,
   write to `artifacts/trial-NNN-<variant>/codex-history/`. Format
   defined below ("Codex history format").
4. **Copy session transcripts.** Pull session jsonls from
   `~/.claude/projects/<draft-dir>/<sessionId>.jsonl` into
   `artifacts/trial-NNN-<variant>/transcripts/`. The session-to-rig
   mapping comes from the stacks dump (which is captured in step 2).
5. **Write archive manifest** at `artifacts/trial-NNN-<variant>/archive-manifest.yaml`
   summarizing what was captured: counts per table, count of commits,
   count of transcripts, archive timestamp, script invocation.
6. **Print next steps.** "Archive complete. Run
   `bin/experiment-cleanup.sh <trial-slug>` to delete disposable surfaces.
   Verify the archive contents at `<sanctum-path>` before running cleanup."

The script is idempotent: re-running overwrites the prior dump (with a
warning). Cleanup refuses to run if archive-manifest is missing or stale
relative to the live state.

## Cleanup script — `experiment-cleanup.sh`

```
bin/experiment-cleanup.sh <trial-slug>
```

### Steps

1. **Verify archive completion.** Refuse to run if
   `artifacts/trial-NNN-<variant>/archive-manifest.yaml` doesn't exist
   or its timestamp is older than the guild dir's most recent
   modification (suggests work happened after archive — re-archive
   before cleanup).
2. **Delete the codex fork.** `gh repo delete <fork> --confirm`. Fail
   loud if the fork has unmerged content the script doesn't recognize
   from the archive (rare, but the safety check matters).
3. **Delete the guild dir.** `rm -rf /workspace/experiments/<trial-dir>`.
4. **Print confirmation.** "Trial <slug> cleanup complete. Sanctum
   artifacts preserved at `<path>`."

The script is **not idempotent** — re-running on a cleaned trial errors
("nothing to clean up").

## Artifact layout

```
experiments/X<NNN>-<slug>/
├── spec.md                                # the experiment's research spec
└── artifacts/
    ├── trial-001-<variant-slug>/
    │   ├── manifest.yaml                  # the canonical trial config
    │   ├── setup-snapshot.yaml            # init-time snapshot
    │   ├── archive-manifest.yaml          # archive-time summary
    │   ├── README.md                      # trial-level prose doc
    │   ├── prompts/                       # variant-specific prompts
    │   │   └── ...
    │   ├── transcripts/                   # per-session jsonls
    │   │   └── <sessionId>.jsonl
    │   ├── stacks-export/                 # JSON dump per table
    │   │   ├── writs.json
    │   │   ├── rigs.json
    │   │   ├── sessions.json
    │   │   ├── clicks.json
    │   │   └── schema-version.txt
    │   ├── codex-history/                 # per-commit diffs + meta
    │   │   ├── commits-manifest.yaml
    │   │   └── 0001-<short-sha>-<slug>.diff
    │   ├── laboratory/                    # Laboratory's observation sink
    │   │   └── <whatever Laboratory writes>
    │   └── analysis/                      # trial-specific analysis (if any)
    │       └── <date>-<slug>.md
    │
    ├── trial-002-<variant-slug>/          # parallel structure per trial
    │   └── ...
    │
    └── analysis/                          # cross-trial analysis
        └── <date>-comparison.md
```

### Notes

- **Trial dir name is `trial-NNN-<variant-slug>`.** NNN is zero-padded;
  variant slug is lowercase kebab-case.
- **`trial-NNN-<variant>/analysis/`** is for analysis specific to one
  trial; **`artifacts/analysis/`** is for cross-trial comparison.
- **`trial-NNN-<variant>/laboratory/`** is where Laboratory writes its
  observations during the trial. Path is captured in the manifest's
  `guild.laboratory_sink`.
- **Findings go in `experiments/X<NNN>-<slug>/findings.md`** at the
  experiment root, not in any trial dir. The findings doc consolidates
  across trials and answers the experiment's research question.

## Stacks export format

One JSON file per table, each containing an array of row objects.

```json
[
  {
    "id": "w-...",
    "type": "mandate",
    "title": "...",
    "body": "...",
    "status": "open",
    "createdAt": "2026-...",
    "...": "..."
  },
  { "id": "w-...", "...": "..." }
]
```

### File-per-table choice

Per-table beats per-row for our use case:

- Typical row sizes are 1-10KB; per-row overhead (filesystem blocks,
  git blob entries) eats meaningful space at this scale.
- Row counts per trial are tens to hundreds — table files stay
  manageable.
- We don't need row-level git history (dumps are snapshots, not
  append-only).
- Analysis instruments load the table once and iterate.

### Carve-out for large content

If any single row carries content >50KB (e.g., a writ body that's a
50KB brief), the dump can extract it to `stacks-export/blobs/<sha>.<ext>`
and replace the inline content with a reference field
(`body_ref: "blobs/<sha>.md"`). Only do this when actually needed; the
default is inline.

### Schema versioning

`stacks-export/schema-version.txt` records the framework SHA the dump
was taken against. Future readers use this to interpret the row schema.

### Default scope

A fresh guild contains only the trial's data, so the default dump is
**every table, every row**. No scope filtering needed. The manifest's
optional `archive_extras` field lets a trial opt into additional tables
beyond the framework defaults (e.g., raw CDC event log) if the
experiment specifically needs them.

## Codex history format

```
codex-history/
├── commits-manifest.yaml
├── 0001-<short-sha>-<slug>.diff
├── 0002-<short-sha>-<slug>.diff
└── ...
```

### `commits-manifest.yaml`

```yaml
base_sha: <40-char>            # the trial's codex base
head_sha: <40-char>            # the fork's main HEAD at archive time
commits:
  - seq: 0001
    sha: <40-char>
    parent: <40-char>
    short: <8-char>
    subject: 'reckoner: sweep vision-keeper references'
    author: 'implementer'
    timestamp: '2026-...'
    rig_id: 'r-...'              # cross-ref into rigs.json
    session_id: '<sessionId>'    # cross-ref into sessions.json + transcripts
    writ_id: 'w-...'             # cross-ref into writs.json
    diff_file: '0001-<short>-<slug>.diff'
  - seq: 0002
    ...
```

### Diff files

Standard git format-patch / git format style; one file per commit. The
filename's seq prefix preserves order; the short SHA + slug aids
human navigation.

## Trial README format

Each trial dir has a `README.md` capturing the trial's narrative.

```markdown
# Trial <NNN> — <variant slug>

## What this trial tests
<one paragraph: which hypothesis or variant config; what we expect>

## Setup
- Nexus branch: <branch> @ <SHA>
- Codex fork: <URL> @ <SHA>
- Plugin deps: see `setup-snapshot.yaml`
- Variant config: <pointer to prompts/ or framework changes>

## Methodology
<what commissions were posted, in what order, what was measured>

## Results
<metrics, comparisons, surprises; reference analysis/ files>

## Verdict
<confirmed / refuted / inconclusive, with one-paragraph rationale>
```

The init script auto-fills **Setup** with resolved SHAs and paths. The
experiment author fills the rest.

## Trial discipline

- **One X### = one experiment with one research question.** Use the
  X### namespace freely. Don't overload one X### with multiple
  experiments.
- **Within one experiment, partition by trial.** Variants of the same
  hypothesis live as separate trial dirs.
- **Heuristic for trial vs. experiment:** same hypothesis → trial; new
  hypothesis → new experiment.
- **Old X### items with multiple experiments** stay as historical
  exceptions. Don't rationalize. Apply the discipline going forward.

## Naming conventions

| thing | format | example |
|---|---|---|
| Experiment slug | `X<NNN>-<lowercase-kebab>` | `X016-p3-orientation-suppression` |
| Nexus experiment branch | `experiment/<experiment-slug>` | `experiment/X016-p3-orientation-suppression` |
| Codex fork repo | `experiment-<experiment-slug>-trial-<NNN>-<variant>` | `experiment-X016-p3-orientation-suppression-trial-001-strong-prompt` |
| Trial dir slug | `<experiment-slug>-trial-<NNN>-<variant>` | (used in `/workspace/experiments/` and as the cleanup arg) |
| Trial artifact dir | `trial-<NNN>-<variant>/` | `trial-001-strong-prompt/` |

Variant slugs use lowercase kebab-case, descriptive but short.

## Worked example: X016 P3 orientation suppression

(Hypothetical — illustrates how the pieces fit together.)

### Experiment

- **Number:** X016
- **Slug:** `X016-p3-orientation-suppression`
- **Research question:** Can a fresh implementer session, given a
  hand-crafted 30K-token handoff, produce productive work in <5 turns?
- **Spec location:** `experiments/X016-p3-orientation-suppression/spec.md`

### Trials

Three prompt variants tested against the same brief (rig 1's docs sweep,
handoff at T52):

| trial | variant | what differs |
|---|---|---|
| 001 | `strong-prompt` | imperative anti-orientation directives ("Do NOT re-read X"), evidence anchors |
| 002 | `mild-prompt` | permissive framing ("X is known-good") |
| 003 | `monolithic-baseline` | no handoff; runs the brief from scratch as control |

### Per-trial flow

1. Author writes `manifest.yaml` for trial-001-strong-prompt.
2. `bin/experiment-init.sh --manifest .../trial-001-strong-prompt/manifest.yaml`
   creates the nexus branch (if not already), forks the codex, generates
   the guild, snapshots setup, stubs the README.
3. Post the brief commission via `nsg commission-post --guild
   /workspace/experiments/X016-p3-orientation-suppression-trial-001-strong-prompt
   --brief .../prompts/handoff-strong.md`.
4. Rig executes; sessions record; Laboratory writes observations to
   the trial's laboratory dir.
5. When the trial completes, `bin/experiment-archive.sh
   X016-p3-orientation-suppression-trial-001-strong-prompt` dumps stacks,
   captures codex commits, copies transcripts, writes archive manifest.
6. Author fills out the trial README with methodology + results +
   verdict.
7. `bin/experiment-cleanup.sh X016-p3-orientation-suppression-trial-001-strong-prompt`
   deletes the fork and guild dir.
8. Repeat for trials 002 and 003.

### Cross-trial analysis

`experiments/X016-p3-orientation-suppression/artifacts/analysis/2026-MM-DD-orientation-comparison.md`
runs an instrument that compares orientation-tax metrics across the
three trials' transcripts and stacks dumps; produces the answer to the
research question. Findings consolidate to
`experiments/X016-p3-orientation-suppression/findings.md`.

## Open implementation questions

These are unresolved in this draft and need addressing before the
infrastructure is built:

1. **`nsg init` invocation.** What does the current command look like?
   Does it accept a path argument for the guild dir, or does it create
   a guild in the current dir? The setup script needs the right
   incantation.
2. **Plugin pinning mechanism.** How does the framework's `package.json`
   express plugin pinning today? By workspace path, by npm version, by
   git ref? The script needs to generate the right shape.
3. **Prompts copy vs. symlink.** Does the framework re-read role
   instructions on each session spawn (allowing symlink-and-edit), or
   only at guild init (requiring copy + re-init for changes)? Affects
   how prompts variation works during a trial.
4. **Stacks dump primitive.** Does the framework provide a clean dump
   command that produces JSON-per-table, or does the archive script
   need to query the DB directly? If the latter, format-stability
   becomes a concern across framework versions.
5. **Laboratory sink config.** How is the Laboratory's output
   destination configured today — guild.json field, env var, plugin
   prop? The init script needs to wire it deliberately.
6. **Session transcript location.** The transcripts live in
   `~/.claude/projects/<draft-dir>/<sessionId>.jsonl` for production
   today. For experimental guilds with non-default draft locations, do
   the transcripts still go there? Affects the archive script's
   transcript-copy step.
7. **`gh` org permissions.** Does the experimental setup user have
   permissions to fork into the upstream codex's org and to create/delete
   forks programmatically? If not, fork-creation needs human-in-loop.
8. **Convention for `archive_extras` table names.** Should the manifest
   reference table names directly, or is there a higher-level set of
   "raw event log," "queue snapshots," etc. that the script knows how
   to extract?

These are pragmatic questions about how the framework's existing
primitives map to what the script needs to do. They're each
straightforward once we look at the framework code; surfacing them
now so the next pass can resolve them.

## Future considerations

- **Multi-fork experiments.** The current spec defaults to one fork per
  trial. Some experiments may need multiple parallel forks (e.g.,
  comparing prompt variants concurrently to save calendar time). Design
  is compatible with this — just init multiple manifests.
- **Long-lived experiments.** Some experiments may run trials over
  weeks (e.g., observing P3 behavior across many real commissions). The
  cleanup discipline needs adjusting for these — possibly an interim
  "snapshot" step that captures state without deleting the fork.
- **Cross-experiment comparison.** When two experiments answer related
  questions (e.g., X016 P3 orientation, X017 P3 trigger heuristics),
  cross-experiment analysis lives in `experiments/analysis/` (or wherever
  the sanctum convention places it). Out of scope for this spec.

One flag worth surfacing before we dig in, since it's structural rather than a section-level critique:

**The spec leans on Laboratory in three places** (`guild.laboratory_sink` in the manifest, the `laboratory/` artifact subdir, and the per-trial flow step "Laboratory writes observations to the trial's laboratory dir"). So either those references want stripping out, or the spec wants a brief replacement story for how trial-side observations get captured beyond what stacks export gives us.

Otherwise — what's on your mind? Some natural starting points if you want a menu:

1. **Three-repo separation & lifecycle** — the foundational shape (sanctum / nexus branch / codex fork)
2. **Manifest schema** — what's in / what's missing / SHA-pinning discipline
3. **The three scripts** (init / archive / cleanup) — steps, idempotency, failure modes
4. **Artifact layout & dump formats** — file-per-table, codex history, trial README
5. **Trial discipline** (one X### = one experiment, trial vs. experiment heuristic)
6. **Open implementation questions** — the eight framework-mapping items

Or just point at a section and we'll go.

**1. Laboratory becomes the home for export tooling.** Three concrete tools fall out of your comments:
- `nsg lab export-books <path>` — dumps every stacks book/table to JSON-per-file at the given path.
- `nsg lab git-range <repo> <base> <head> <output>` — generic git-range diff dump (also useful beyond this infra).
- (Implied) `nsg lab archive <trial-dir>` — the orchestrator that calls the above + writes status, so the bash script is mostly a thin wrapper.

This is cleaner than the standalone scripts walking the DB directly. Promotion to `stacks` plugin if proven — agreed.

**2. Setup uses standard guild commands instead of file generation.** The init flow becomes:
   1. `nsg init <guild-dir>`
   2. for each plugin: `nsg plugin install <name>@<git-ref>`
   3. `nsg codex add <repo-url> --sha <sha>`
   4. merge `guild.config` from manifest into `guild.json`
   5. copy `guild.files` entries
   6. record `status.laboratory.setup`

Drops the bespoke guild.json / package.json generation entirely.

**3. Manifest restructured around guild as umbrella.**

```yaml
experiment: X016-...
trial: 001
variant: strong-prompt

nexus:
  branch: experiment/X016-...
  base_sha: ...

codex:
  upstream_org: shardworks
  upstream_repo: nexus
  base_sha: ...

guild:
  plugins:
    - { name: '@shardworks/clerk-apparatus', ref: <git-ref> }
  config:                       # merged into guild.json
    <partial guild.json>
  files:
    - { sourcePath: prompts/handoff-strong.md,
        guildPath: animator/instructions/implement.md }

status:                         # nexus-style status record, grows over time
  laboratory:
    phase: new                  # default if omitted
    setup: { ... }              # filled at init
    archive: { ... }            # filled at archive
```

`prompts_dir` and `laboratory_sink` both dissolve. `archive_extras` dropped.

**4. Codex repo is a fresh repo, not a fork.** `gh repo create` + push. No upstream metadata pollution.

**5. Transcripts come from the animator book.** No `~/.claude/projects/` dependency — the books export already carries them. Drops the whole "copy session jsonls" archive step.

**6. `seq` field dropped from `commits-manifest.yaml`** — YAML sequence position carries it.

## Follow-up questions

**A. Live observation vs. export-only.** With Laboratory pivoting to export tools called at archive time, nothing observes *during* the trial — Laboratory is silent until you ask it to dump. Is that what you intend? Or should Laboratory still write observations live (and the export tools are an addition)? My read is the former — guild books already capture the live state, and Laboratory's job is now to *project* that state into sanctum-shaped artifacts on demand.

**B. Per-trial vs. per-experiment nexus branching.** Your comment leans toward letting trials need their own branches. Concrete proposal: don't enforce a relationship — each manifest just declares whatever `branch + base_sha` it wants. Trials in the same experiment that share framework state share a branch; trials that need framework variance get their own. No rule. Acceptable?

**C. Status namespacing.** You proposed `status.laboratory.{phase, setup, archive}`. The setup/archive entries are clearly Laboratory's outputs. But `phase` (new/active/completed) is about the *trial's* lifecycle, not Laboratory's. Two options:
   - **Option 1.** All under `status.laboratory.*` — single namespace, accept that `phase` is logically trial-level but lives there for simplicity.
   - **Option 2.** `status.trial.phase` + `status.laboratory.{setup, archive}` — separates concerns: trial-level state vs. plugin-specific records.
   
   I lean Option 2; you?

**D. Manifest mutability.** Status grows over time (`phase` flips, `setup` and `archive` get filled in). Does the manifest become read-write — one document with both immutable inputs and growing status? Or does manifest stay immutable and status live in a sibling file (`status.yaml`)? Also, when archive overwrites `status.laboratory.archive`, do we preserve prior runs?

**E. Worked-example experiment.** You want to see an existing multi-hypothesis experiment mapped onto the new structure. Most past experiments were paper analyses, which this infra doesn't govern. Candidates that would fit:
   - **P3 (the actual driver)** — orientation suppression, trigger heuristics, granularity, cost confirmation as separate experiments under one umbrella.
   - **Cost-optimization landscape** — many of the 29 ideas could become trials.
   - **X005 / X007 / X011** if any actually had framework-touching multi-hypothesis structure (I'd need to look).
   
   Which do you want me to walk through?

## Order of attack

I'd suggest:
1. **Lock the manifest shape** (changes 2, 3, 4 + follow-ups C, D) — it's load-bearing for everything else.
2. **Resolve Laboratory's role** (change 1 + follow-up A) — affects script structure.
3. **Worked example** (follow-up E) — validates the shape against a real case.
4. **Per-trial branching** (follow-up B) — cheap to confirm.
5. **Sweep the spec** to integrate.

Which piece do you want to dig into first?

content.text:
## Confirmations

**A.** Locked: Laboratory exports, doesn't observe live.

**B.** Locked: per-trial branching by default; manifest uses `version` (not `git-ref`) so non-nexus plugins compose naturally.

**C.** Got it — plugin-ID is the key, no synthetic `trial` namespace. Since Laboratory owns init and archive, everything (`phase`, `setup`, `archive`) sits under `laboratory`:

```yaml
status:
  laboratory:
    phase: new
    setup: { ... }
    archive: { ... }
```

**D.** Going with `trial.yaml` (immutable inputs) + `trial-status.yaml` (mutable record). Worth the split — keeps the inputs hashable / diffable and the status mutations clean. Archive overwrites in place; no preservation.

## On (E) — the real question

Honest answer: **the one-hypothesis-per-experiment discipline is mostly an organizational convenience, not a methodological requirement, and real science does not enforce it.** What real science actually disciplines is something different.

**How real science actually handles multi-hypothesis work:**

1. **Factorial designs.** Vary multiple factors simultaneously; analyze main effects and interactions. A 2×3 design tests 6 conditions and tells you about 2 factors plus their interaction in one trial — more efficient than 6 separate experiments. Standard in agricultural science, psychology, ML evals.
2. **Multi-arm trials.** Clinical research routinely tests several interventions vs. a control in one study (e.g., the RECOVERY trial during COVID tested ~10 treatments against a shared control arm).
3. **Pre-registered hypothesis families.** Labs lock in multiple related hypotheses *before* running the study. The discipline is the pre-registration, not the partition.
4. **Primary vs. secondary endpoints.** One pre-declared primary question owns the verdict; secondary endpoints are reported as exploratory.

The actual disciplines that matter:

- **No HARKing** (Hypothesizing After Results are Known). Don't spawn new hypotheses mid-experiment to fit data you've already seen.
- **Multiple comparisons correction.** If you tested 20 hypotheses, your significance bar moves.
- **Report everything, including nulls.** No cherry-picking the hypothesis that worked.
- **Distinguish exploratory from confirmatory.** Different epistemic claims, different rigor.

**Translated for our context:**

The "one X### = one experiment with one research question" rule is *limiting* if read strictly, because:
- A single trial setup often answers multiple questions for free (a handoff-suppression trial generates data on cost cliffs, quality cliffs, and turn-count distributions all at once).
- Forcing artificial partition wastes setup work.
- Real research often has a primary question AND secondary observations from the same data.

But it's *useful* as an organizational anchor:
- Each X### has one *primary* verdict — easy to navigate, easy to write findings.
- Avoids slop where an experiment loses its center.

**Suggestion: relax the rule, keep the spirit.**

- Each experiment has **one primary research question** (the verdict it must answer).
- It MAY record **secondary findings** from the same data, clearly labelled as such — they're bonus, not the verdict.
- Don't spawn new primary questions mid-experiment. If a real new question emerges from the data, that's a new experiment with its own pre-registered hypothesis (anti-HARKing discipline).

That gives you navigability without forcing artificial partition. It also matches what serious labs do: one preregistered primary endpoint, multiple secondary endpoints reported as exploratory.

Want me to fold that framing into the spec's "Trial discipline" section?

## Where to go next

Order of attack from before still works. Now that the manifest shape is locked and (E) is answered, the remaining live threads are:

1. Worked example using P3 (the real driver) — does the shape hold up when you actually try to write a trial.yaml for it?
2. Sweep the spec to integrate all of the above.

Want me to write a P3-orientation-suppression `trial.yaml` against the new shape as the integration test? Or go straight to the full spec sweep?

content.text:
## On vocabulary and structure

Your reframing — "do something with controlled+manipulated variables, capture everything produced" — is cleaner than the trial framing, and exposes that **"trial" carries scientific baggage we don't really earn.** A "trial" connotes hypothesis-testing, comparison groups, statistical inference. What we actually have is an experimental apparatus that you set up, run, and capture.

I'd suggest **"run"** as the swap. It's what scientists call this when stripped of the comparative-trial frame:

- An **experiment** is the inquiry: research question + analytical frame.
- A **run** is one execution of the apparatus under a specific set of variable settings.
- An **analysis** looks across runs (or at one, if there's only one) and produces findings.

`trial.yaml` → `run.yaml`. `trial-001-strong-prompt/` → `run-001-strong-prompt/`. `experiment-init.sh` stays (it inits one run; the script is per-run already). `experiment-archive.sh` becomes `run-archive.sh`. Etc.

Other candidates I considered and rejected:
- **specimen** — accurate (captured artifact) but overstylized
- **capture** — describes the output, not the act
- **execution** — bureaucratic
- **take** — too cute

"Run" it is, unless you want to push back.

## Does hypothesis count or type matter?

**No — hypotheses are the analytical frame, runs are the execution frame.** They live in different layers and don't have to map 1:1.

- One run can illuminate several hypotheses (a handoff-suppression run produces data relevant to cost, quality, turn-count, and seal-conflict hypotheses simultaneously).
- One hypothesis often needs several runs to confirm (vary one parameter, replicate, compare).
- Some experiments have one hypothesis; some have a primary + secondaries; some are exploratory with no formal hypothesis at all.

The structure is hypothesis-agnostic. What it cares about is: how many runs, what variables differ between them, how the analysis stitches them together. The hypothesis count just shapes how the findings doc is written.

This means **the run discipline is simpler than the trial discipline I'd drafted.** It just says: each run is a single execution with a specific variable setting; runs go in `run-NNN-<variant>/`; everything captured for that run goes in there.

## Updated discipline section — proposed

Replacing the existing "Trial discipline" section:

> ## Experiment discipline
>
> An experiment is one piece of inquiry — a research question plus the
> analytical frame to answer it. An experiment produces one or more
> **runs**, each capturing what happened when the apparatus was set up
> and executed with a specific set of variable settings.
>
> ### Experiments
> - **One experiment has one primary research question** — the verdict
>   it must answer. The findings doc resolves to that question.
> - **Secondary findings are allowed and welcome** — analyses of the
>   same captured data that illuminate other questions. Label them as
>   secondary; they don't dilute the verdict.
> - **No HARKing.** New primary questions that emerge during analysis
>   become new experiments with their own pre-registered question.
>   Don't retrofit a question to fit data you've already seen.
> - Number of runs per experiment is whatever the analysis needs.
>   Could be one (qualitative, observational) or many (varying parameters,
>   replicating).
>
> ### Runs
> - **A run is a single execution of the apparatus** with a specific set
>   of variable settings. It produces a captured-state artifact set in
>   `run-NNN-<variant>/`.
> - **Variables split into controlled and manipulated.** Controlled
>   variables are held constant across runs (or across an experiment).
>   Manipulated variables are what each run varies. Both go in
>   `run.yaml`.
> - **Replicates are runs with identical variable settings** — useful
>   when checking for non-determinism or noise. Treat as ordinary runs
>   with a `replicate-of` field if needed; don't engineer special
>   structure now.

## Structural impacts

Mostly cosmetic — the substance of the spec doesn't change. The sweep:
1. `trial` → `run` throughout (file names, directory names, doc headers, script names where applicable, script arguments, manifest paths).
2. Replace "Trial discipline" with the relaxed "Experiment discipline" above.
3. Replace "Trial manifest format" with "Run manifest format" — same content.
4. Replace "Trial README format" with "Run README format" — same template.
5. Worked example uses "runs" not "trials".

**Status namespacing unaffected** — `status.laboratory.{phase, setup, archive}` is run-level, not trial/hypothesis-level.

**Manifest shape unaffected** — already designed around variables (controlled framework version + codex SHA, manipulated prompts/config), not around hypothesis-level structure.

## Open ask

Two confirmations before I sweep:

1. **"Run" as the term** — yes, or push back?
2. **Relaxed discipline section as drafted** — wording OK, or want changes before it goes in?

Once those are settled I can do the full spec sweep in one pass.

# Experimental infrastructure: setup, artifacts, lifecycle

**Status:** draft (2026-04-30) — pending Sean's review.

## Purpose

This document specifies the infrastructure for running framework-side
experiments — trials that need a controlled codex, controlled framework
state, isolated session recording, and reproducible setup. The immediate
driver is P3 (engine-pipeline-decomposition; click `c-modxxtu6`), whose
sub-questions (orientation suppression, trigger heuristics, granularity,
cost confirmation) all require running real implementer sessions in
isolated conditions before the architecture can be designed empirically.
Subsequent experiments touching framework or commission-pipeline behavior
will reuse the same infrastructure.

The infrastructure does **not** govern paper analyses (instruments run
against existing transcript archives) — those need no setup. It governs
trials that **execute new commissions** under controlled conditions.

## Design principles

1. **Three-repo separation.** The sanctum, the framework (nexus), and the
   codex live in three repos with distinct persistence rules. Each artifact
   has one canonical home.
2. **One guild per trial.** Every trial gets its own fresh guild and its
   own short-lived codex fork. This eliminates trial-isolation problems
   inside the guild's books — every row in the guild DB belongs to this
   trial by construction.
3. **Sanctum is the canonical record.** Methodology, briefs, prompts,
   captured execution data, and analysis live in the sanctum. The other
   two repos (nexus branches, codex forks) are reproducibility breadcrumbs.
4. **Reproducibility via SHA pinning.** Every framework dep, every codex
   base, every plugin version is pinned to a SHA in a per-trial manifest
   that lives in the sanctum.
5. **Disposable execution surfaces.** Codex forks and live guild dirs are
   short-lived. They're created at trial start, archived to sanctum at
   trial end, and deleted. Re-running an experiment doesn't depend on
   long-lived disposable state.

## Architecture

### The three repos

| repo | role | persistence |
|---|---|---|
| **Sanctum** (`nexus-mk2`) | methodology, manifests, prompts, captured artifacts, analysis, findings | indefinite; canonical research record |
| **Nexus framework** | only the framework changes that produce experimental variance. One branch per experiment (not per trial) — a single experiment-wide framework state that all variants in that experiment share. | indefinite; archived branches |
| **Codex fork** | short-lived complete fork of the codex repo, one per trial. The fork's `main` branch is the trial's working ref; commits there are the rigs' actual edits. | short-lived; captured-then-deleted |
